profile.name: Margaret
profile.role_in_household: Primary client; single household head managing finances post-divorce
profile.financial_literacy_level: Moderate-to-high: comfortable discussing investments and taxes at a big-picture level, but may not track every account detail or statement line item month-to-month.
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: Growth-oriented and willing to tolerate market swings if the plan feels intentional; tends to take calculated risks when she understands the rationale, but gets cautious when cash flow feels tight or uncertain.
profile.communication_style: Direct but conversational; uses qualifiers like "I think" and "roughly" when recalling numbers, asks clarifying questions, and prefers a quick summary first with details second. Will occasionally pause to do mental math and may circle back to confirm she heard correctly.
emotional_tendencies: mild anxiety around financial obligations tied to the divorce
relief when there is a clear checklist and timeline
profile.decision_making_style: Decides after hearing 2–3 options with pros/cons; likes to sleep on bigger moves, but will act quickly on straightforward administrative items once she trusts the process.
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Cautiously optimistic: open to guidance, but wants transparency on fees, assumptions, and what is and isn’t guaranteed.
internal_biases: recency bias (recent market moves influence comfort level)
mental accounting (separates "retirement" money from "accessible" money and is reluctant to mix purposes)
hidden_concerns: maintaining lifestyle while covering fixed obligations and expenses without a second income
worry about being overexposed to one or two accounts/providers without realizing it
concern about taxes and making an avoidable mistake at her bracket
